questions.text: A return-air duct must pass through a fire-rated corridor wall assembly. What does this generally require?

options.t: Listed/labeled duct materials or construction meeting the flame-spread/smoke-developed limits for that application, or a fire/smoke damper at the penetration
Nothing special — any galvanized or flexible duct is acceptable through any rated assembly
options.why: Rated assemblies impose specific material/construction and penetration-protection requirements — "any duct will do" is exactly the assumption that gets flagged on inspection.

options.t: Only the penetration needs fireproofing; the duct material itself is irrelevant once past the wall
options.why: Both the penetration detail AND the duct material/construction matter for rated-assembly and plenum applications, not just the hole in the wall.

options.t: The requirement only applies to supply ducts, not return ducts
options.why: Fire/smoke-rated assembly requirements apply based on what the duct passes through, not whether it is supply or return.

questions.why: Ducts through rated assemblies or serving as plenums must use listed/labeled materials meeting applicable flame-spread/smoke-developed limits, or be protected with fire/smoke dampers at the penetration — a common area where inspectors catch shortcuts.
questions.ref: Mechanical code duct-construction/fire-safety provisions (IMC-style Ch. 6 concept area)

questions.text: A supply duct runs directly adjacent to a gas-fired furnace heat exchanger before transitioning to insulated duct further downstream. What is the code-relevant concern at that first section?

options.t: The duct needs the same kind of clearance-to-combustibles (or approved thermal protection) that applies near the heat source
None — sheet-metal duct is inherently fireproof, so proximity to the heat exchanger is not a concern
options.why: "Fireproof duct material" does not eliminate the clearance requirement — the concern is heat transfer to nearby combustibles through/around the duct assembly, not the duct catching fire itself.

options.t: Clearance requirements only apply to the furnace cabinet, not to ductwork connected to it
options.why: Clearance logic follows the heat source through the connected ductwork in that first section, not just the appliance cabinet boundary.

options.t: Insulating the duct at that point would fix any clearance concern
options.why: Adding insulation to a duct in a clearance-reduction situation is not a substitute for meeting the actual required clearance or using an approved/listed clearance-reduction method.

questions.why: Ductwork near heat-producing equipment can carry the same clearance-to-combustibles logic that applies to the appliance itself — a frequent retrofit-inspection citation when ducts get rerouted close to heat sources.

questions.text: Why do mechanical codes require ducts to be sealed at joints and adequately supported, beyond just "good practice"?

options.t: Unsealed/poorly supported ducts leak conditioned air and can sag or separate over time — both a code-compliance and an energy-code issue
Sealing and support are purely cosmetic concerns with no code basis
options.why: Duct sealing and support intersect both mechanical code airflow/safety intent and energy-code efficiency requirements — it is a real compliance item, not cosmetics.

options.t: They are required only for ducts visible to the occupant, not concealed ducts in attics/crawlspaces
options.why: Concealed ducts are actually where poor sealing/support causes the most energy loss and sag-related failures — the requirement is not limited to visible runs.

options.t: Support requirements exist only to prevent noise, unrelated to airflow or energy performance
options.why: Support prevents sagging/joint separation that causes air leakage and reduced system performance — noise control is a secondary benefit, not the driving requirement.

questions.why: Proper duct sealing and support limit air leakage (a major, well-documented source of HVAC energy waste) and prevent sagging/joint separation over time — both are enforced through mechanical and energy code provisions, not just best-practice advice.
questions.ref: Mechanical code duct construction/sealing provisions; energy code duct-sealing requirements

questions.text: What is a common code-relevant limitation on flexible duct installation that a rushed attic install often violates?

options.t: Maximum allowable length and avoiding excessive compression, kinks, or sagging that restrict airflow
There is no limitation at all — flexible duct can be run at any length or routing
options.why: Flex duct has real limitations: excess length, compression and sagging are common (and code-relevant) failure modes that choke airflow.

options.t: The only limitation is color/labeling of the outer jacket
options.why: Labeling matters for identification, but the functional/code concern is length, support spacing, and avoiding kinks/compression that restrict airflow.

options.t: Flexible duct may never be used to connect to a supply register under any circumstance
options.why: Flex duct is commonly used for register connections when installed within its length/support limitations — it is not categorically prohibited there.

questions.why: Flexible duct installation is limited by allowable length and proper support spacing, and it must avoid compression, kinks, or excessive sag — all of which choke airflow and represent common, code-relevant field failures.
questions.ref: Mechanical code flexible-duct installation provisions (IMC-style Ch. 6 concept area)

questions.text: You need to run low-voltage control cable through a ceiling cavity that serves as a return-air plenum. What applies?

options.t: The cable and any exposed materials must be plenum-rated / limited-combustible to meet flame-spread and smoke limits
Any standard jacketed cable is fine as long as it is out of sight
options.why: Return-air plenums require plenum-rated cable and limited-combustible materials — ordinary jacketed cable is a common violation.

options.t: Plenum rules only apply to the sheet-metal ducts, not to a ceiling cavity used as a plenum
options.why: A ceiling cavity used to convey return air IS a plenum for code purposes, so materials in it must meet plenum requirements.

options.t: Nothing applies because control cable carries no air
options.why: The concern is smoke/flame spread of materials exposed to moving air, regardless of what the cable carries.

questions.why: A ceiling cavity used to move return air is a plenum; materials exposed in it (cable, insulation, etc.) must meet flame-spread/smoke-developed limits — hence plenum-rated cable and limited-combustible materials.
questions.ref: Mechanical code plenum provisions (IMC-style Ch. 6 concept area)

questions.text: Which statement about duct insulation under the mechanical/energy codes is correct?

options.t: Ducts in unconditioned spaces generally require insulation to a specified R-value, and the insulation/jacket must meet flame-spread and smoke limits
Duct insulation is purely optional and never code-mandated
options.why: Ducts in unconditioned spaces have code-required insulation R-values (energy code), and the materials must meet fire-safety limits.

options.t: Any insulation material may be used regardless of flame-spread rating
options.why: Duct insulation and jackets must meet flame-spread/smoke-developed limits — material choice is constrained, not free.

options.t: Only supply ducts need insulation; return ducts never do
options.why: Insulation requirements depend on the surrounding conditions (e.g., unconditioned space), applying to return ducts there as well.

questions.why: Ducts in unconditioned spaces require insulation to code-specified R-values (energy code), and the insulation and its jacket must meet flame-spread/smoke-developed limits — both a thermal and a fire-safety requirement.

questions.text: A grease-laden commercial kitchen exhaust duct is being planned like an ordinary sheet-metal return. Why is that wrong?

options.t: Grease ducts have their own stricter rules — welded liquid-tight construction, clearances/enclosure, cleanouts and slope — distinct from general exhaust
There is no difference; all exhaust ducts follow the same construction rules
options.why: Grease exhaust is a distinct, heavily regulated class (welded, cleanouts, fire clearance) — not the same as a general return or bathroom exhaust.

options.t: Grease ducts only need a filter at the hood; the duct itself is unregulated
options.why: The grease DUCT construction (liquid-tight welds, access, clearances) is specifically regulated because of fire risk, beyond the hood filter.

options.t: Grease ducts can share a chase with general exhaust to save space
options.why: Grease ducts have dedicated construction and clearance/enclosure rules and generally cannot be casually combined with other exhaust.

questions.why: Commercial grease exhaust is a distinct, tightly regulated class: liquid-tight welded construction, required clearances/enclosure, cleanouts and proper slope — reflecting its fire hazard, unlike a general environmental-exhaust duct.
questions.ref: Mechanical code grease-duct provisions (IMC-style Ch. 5/6 concept area)
